questions.question:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in L1 cache and a hit in L2 cache, a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the time taken for this transfer?

A. 2 nanoseconds
B. 20 nanoseconds
C. 22 nanoseconds
D. 88 nanoseconds
questions.to_find: Time to transfer a block from L2 cache to L1 cache on an L1 miss and L2 hit.

understand.plain: The processor checks L1 first. On a miss it checks L2. On an L2 hit, a 4-word block (L1 block size) is transferred from L2 to L1. The transfer time is: time to read from L2 (20 ns) plus time to write into L1 (2 ns), giving 22 ns total.

keywords.explain: Requested data not found in a cache level; system must look at the next (slower) level.
keywords.example: CPU requests address X; X not in L1 (miss), so L2 is checked.

keywords.explain: When data is found at a lower level, an entire cache block (not just the requested word) is moved up to fill the higher-level cache.
keywords.example: L1 block = 4 words, so 4 words are moved from L2 to L1 on a miss.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='440' height='110'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:16px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='70' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CPU</text><rect x='110' y='30' width='80' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L1</text><text x='150' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2 ns | 4 w</text><rect x='220' y='30' width='80' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L2</text><text x='260' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>20 ns | 16 w</text><rect x='330' y='30' width='80' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>MM</text><text x='370' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>200 ns</text><line x1='80' y1='55' x2='110' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='190' y1='55' x2='220' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='300' y1='55' x2='330' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>L2 --20ns read + 2ns write--> L1 = 22 ns</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory hierarchy: CPU -> L1 (2 ns, 4-word block) -> L2 (20 ns, 16-word block) -> MM (200 ns). On L1 miss + L2 hit: block moves from L2 to L1 taking 20+2=22 ns.

given.aim: Find the time to transfer a 4-word block from L2 to L1 on an L1 miss and L2 hit.

terms.meaning: Access time for L1 cache (time to write a block into L1).
terms.example: A hit in L1 costs 2 ns for the processor to get data.
terms.connects: Final step: writing the transferred block into L1.

terms.meaning: Access time for L2 cache (time to read a block from L2).
terms.example: On an L2 hit, reading a block from L2 takes 20 ns.
terms.connects: First step: L2 must be read to supply the 4-word block to L1.

terms.meaning: The amount of data transferred from L2 to L1 on a miss.
terms.example: Even if only 1 word is needed, 4 words are fetched as one L1 block.
terms.connects: Determines the granularity of the transfer; modeled as one block access at each level.

given.plan: On L1 miss, L2 hit: read one block from L2 (t_L2 = 20 ns) then write it into L1 (t_L1 = 2 ns). Total = t_L2 + t_L1.

steps.title: Identify the two operations required
steps.apply: Step A: Read the 4-word block from L2 (one block-level access) = 20 ns. Step B: Write the block into L1 = 2 ns.
steps.note: The transfer is modeled as one block access at each level, independent of the number of words.

steps.title: Calculate total transfer time
steps.note: Option D (88 ns) would arise from 4*(20+2) treating the transfer word-by-word, but that model is used for two-level misses in Q2.4.30, not here.

questions.formula_note: Single-level miss-hit transfer time: time = t_(source level) + t_(destination level). For L2->L1: 20 + 2 = 22 ns.

questions.question:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in both L1 cache and L2 cache, first a block is transferred from the main memory to L2 cache, and then a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the total time taken for the two transfers?

A. 222 nanoseconds
B. 888 nanoseconds
C. 902 nanoseconds
D. 968 nanoseconds
questions.to_find: Total time for two transfers: MM to L2, then L2 to L1, on misses in both L1 and L2.

understand.plain: When both L1 and L2 miss, the system must bring data from main memory into L2, then from L2 into L1. The key insight is that the MM-to-L2 transfer moves data word by word (for 4 words, the L1 block size), each word costing t_MM + t_L2. Then the L2-to-L1 block transfer costs t_L2 + t_L1 as one block access. Total = 4*(200+20) + (20+2) = 880 + 22 = 902 ns.

keywords.explain: Neither L1 nor L2 holds the required block; data must be fetched from main memory, the slowest level.
keywords.example: CPU misses L1, then misses L2, so main memory is accessed.

keywords.explain: In this cache hierarchy model, transferring data from MM to L2 is done one word at a time, each word taking t_MM + t_L2 ns. The number of words transferred equals the L1 block size (4 words).
keywords.example: 4 words * (200+20) ns/word = 880 ns for the MM->L2 stage.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:15px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:12px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='60' height='45' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CPU</text><rect x='100' y='30' width='70' height='45' class='bg'/><text x='135' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L1</text><text x='135' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2ns 4w</text><rect x='200' y='30' width='70' height='45' class='bg'/><text x='235' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L2</text><text x='235' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>20ns 16w</text><rect x='300' y='30' width='70' height='45' class='bg'/><text x='335' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>MM</text><text x='335' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>200ns</text><line x1='70' y1='52' x2='100' y2='52' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='52' x2='200' y2='52' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='270' y1='52' x2='300' y2='52' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='230' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Step 1: MM -> L2: 4*(200+20)=880 ns</text><text x='230' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Step 2: L2 -> L1: 20+2=22 ns</text><text x='230' y='136' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Total: 902 ns</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-step miss: Step 1 fetches 4 words from MM to L2 (each word: 200+20=220 ns, total 880 ns). Step 2 transfers the 4-word block from L2 to L1 (20+2=22 ns). Grand total = 902 ns.

given.aim: Compute total time for MM->L2 and L2->L1 transfers on a two-level miss.

terms.meaning: Time to access one word from main memory.
terms.example: Each word fetched from MM costs 200 ns.
terms.connects: Dominates the first transfer stage; repeated 4 times for L1 block size.

terms.meaning: Access time for L2 cache (per word write from MM, and per block read to L1).
terms.example: Writing each word into L2 costs 20 ns; reading a block out of L2 also costs 20 ns.

terms.meaning: Access time for L1 cache (block write into L1).
terms.example: Loading the 4-word block into L1 takes 2 ns.

terms.meaning: The number of words that form one L1 cache block, which drives the MM->L2 word count.
terms.example: 4 words transferred word-by-word from MM to L2.
terms.connects: Determines the multiplier (4) in the first transfer stage.

given.plan: Transfer 1 (MM->L2): move 4 words (L1 block size) word-by-word; each word costs t_MM + t_L2. Transfer 2 (L2->L1): one block transfer costs t_L2 + t_L1. Total = 4*(t_MM + t_L2) + (t_L2 + t_L1).

steps.title: Transfer 1: Main Memory to L2 (word-by-word)
steps.note: The multiplier is L1 block size (4 words), not L2 block size (16 words), because we only need to fill one L1 block worth of data.

steps.title: Transfer 2: L2 to L1 (block transfer)
steps.note: This is a single block-level access, consistent with the answer to Q2.4.29.

steps.title: Compute total time
steps.note: Option B (888) arises from 4*(200+20+2)=4*222 treating the full path per-word without a separate block transfer for L2->L1. Option D (968) = 4*(200+20)+4*(20+2)=880+88, treating L2->L1 also word-by-word.

questions.formula_note: Two-level miss total time: L1_block_size*(t_MM + t_L2) + (t_L2 + t_L1) = 4*(200+20) + (20+2) = 902 ns. Combines word-by-word MM->L2 fill with block-level L2->L1 transfer.

questions.question:
An 8 KB direct-mapped write-back cache is organized as multiple blocks, each of size 32-bytes. The processor generates 32-bit addresses. The cache controller contains the tag information for each cache block comprising of the following:
- 1 valid bit
- 1 modified bit
- As many bits as the minimum needed to identify the memory block mapped in the cache.

What is the total size of the cache tag directory in bits?

A. 4864 bits
B. 6144 bits
C. 4656 bits
D. 5376 bits
questions.to_find: Total size of the cache tag directory (all tag entries) in bits.

understand.plain: The cache has 256 lines (8 KB / 32 B). Each line's tag entry has a valid bit, a modified bit, and the minimum tag bits needed to distinguish which memory block is in that cache line. The tag bits = 32 - index_bits - offset_bits = 32 - 8 - 5 = 19 bits. Total = 256 lines * (1 + 1 + 19) = 256 * 21 = 5376 bits.

keywords.explain: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line; line index = (block number) mod (number of lines).
keywords.example: 256-line cache: memory block 257 maps to cache line 1.

keywords.explain: High-order address bits stored alongside data so the cache can verify whether a hit or miss has occurred.
keywords.example: 32-bit address, 8 index bits, 5 offset bits => tag = 19 bits.

keywords.explain: Set to 1 when a cache block has been written but not yet flushed to main memory (needed for write-back policy).
keywords.example: Block is written; dirty bit=1. On eviction, write block back to MM.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:15px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:13px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='15' width='160' height='38' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tag: 19 bits</text><text x='90' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(bits 31-13)</text><rect x='170' y='15' width='120' height='38' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Index: 8 bits</text><text x='230' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(bits 12-5)</text><rect x='290' y='15' width='120' height='38' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Offset: 5 bits</text><text x='350' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(bits 4-0)</text><text x='230' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>32-bit address = 19 + 8 + 5</text><rect x='10' y='92' width='30' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='25' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>V</text><rect x='40' y='92' width='30' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='55' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>M</text><rect x='70' y='92' width='130' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='135' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Tag (19 bits)</text><text x='290' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Per line: 21 bits | 256 lines = 5376 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address: 19-bit tag | 8-bit index (256 lines) | 5-bit offset (32 bytes). Per cache line tag entry: 1 valid + 1 modified + 19 tag = 21 bits. Total tag directory = 256 * 21 = 5376 bits.

given.aim: Compute total tag directory size = num_lines * bits_per_entry.

terms.meaning: Total data capacity of the cache.
terms.connects: Divided by block size to get number of cache lines.
Determines offset field width and total block count.

terms.meaning: All physical addresses are 32 bits wide.

terms.term: Overhead bits per line = valid + modified = 2 bits
terms.meaning: Each line also needs 1 valid bit (is the data current?) and 1 modified bit (has it been written to?).
terms.example: Write-back cache needs modified bit; write-through does not.
terms.connects: Added to tag bits to form the total per-line entry width.

given.plan: 1. num_lines = 8192/32 = 256. 2. index_bits = log_2(256) = 8. 3. offset_bits = 5. 4. tag_bits = 32 - 8 - 5 = 19. 5. per_entry = 19+1+1 = 21 bits. 6. total = 256*21 = 5376 bits.

steps.title: Determine address field widths
steps.formula_raw: offset = log_2(block_size); index = log_2(num_lines); tag = 32 - offset - index
steps.apply: offset = log_2(32) = 5 bits. index = log_2(256) = 8 bits. tag = 32 - 5 - 8 = 19 bits.
steps.note: 19 bits is the minimum needed to identify which memory block occupies a cache line.

steps.title: Compute bits per tag entry
steps.note: The question explicitly includes valid bit and modified bit in the tag directory.

steps.title: Compute total tag directory size
steps.note: Option A (4864) = 256*19 ignores the valid and modified bits. Option D (5376) is correct.

questions.formula_note: Tag directory total size = num_lines * (tag_bits + valid_bit + dirty_bit), where tag_bits = address_bits - log_2(num_lines) - log_2(block_size). Here: 256*(19+1+1) = 5376 bits.

questions.question:
A computer has a 256 KB, 4-way set associative, write back data cache with block size of 32 Bytes. The processor sends 32 bit addresses to the cache controller. Each cache tag directory entry contains, in addition to address tag, 2 valid bits, 1 modified bit and 1 replacement bit.

The number of bits in the tag field of an address is

A. 11
B. 14
C. 16
D. 27
questions.to_find: Number of bits in the tag field of the 32-bit address for the given set-associative cache.

understand.plain: In a 4-way set-associative cache, the address is split into tag, set-index, and block-offset. The number of sets = total_blocks / 4 = (256 KB / 32 B) / 4 = 8192 / 4 = 2048 sets. Index bits = log_2(2048) = 11, offset bits = log_2(32) = 5, so tag bits = 32 - 11 - 5 = 16 bits.

keywords.explain: The cache is divided into sets, each with 4 cache lines (ways). A memory block maps to a specific set but can occupy any of the 4 ways within that set.
keywords.example: If there are 2048 sets and 4 ways, total blocks = 2048*4 = 8192.

keywords.explain: Address bits that select which set in the cache to look in. Number of index bits = log_2(number of sets).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:15px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:12px;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='180' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tag: 16 bits</text><text x='100' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bits 31..16</text><rect x='190' y='20' width='130' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='255' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Set Index: 11 bits</text><text x='255' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bits 15..5 (2048 sets)</text><rect x='320' y='20' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Offset: 5 bits</text><text x='380' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>bits 4..0 (32 B)</text><text x='230' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>32-bit address = 16 + 11 + 5</text><text x='230' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total blocks = 256KB/32B = 8192; Sets = 8192/4 = 2048</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address split: 16-bit tag | 11-bit set index (2048 sets) | 5-bit block offset (32 bytes). Total blocks = 256 KB / 32 B = 8192; sets = 8192 / 4 = 2048 => index = 11 bits; tag = 32-11-5 = 16 bits.

given.aim: Find tag bits = 32 - index_bits - offset_bits for a 4-way set associative cache.

terms.connects: Determines total number of cache blocks.

terms.example: 4 ways per set means 4 separate blocks can map to each set without conflict.

terms.connects: Determines offset bits and total block count.

given.plan: 1. total_blocks = 256 KB / 32 B. 2. num_sets = total_blocks / 4. 3. index = log_2(num_sets). 4. offset = log_2(32). 5. tag = 32 - index - offset.

steps.title: Compute total number of cache blocks
Compute offset bits and tag bits
steps.formula_raw: offset = log_2(block_size); tag = address_bits - index - offset
steps.note: The question only asks for the tag field of the ADDRESS, not the size of the full tag directory entry (which also includes 2 valid + 1 modified + 1 replacement = 4 extra bits).

questions.formula_note: Address tag bits for set-associative cache: tag = addr_bits - log_2(num_sets) - log_2(block_size), where num_sets = (cache_size / block_size) / associativity. Here: 32 - log_2(2048) - log_2(32) = 32-11-5 = 16.
